aliases: Braised Chicken with Onion and Mushroom

english: Braised Chicken with Onion and Mushroom

food_subgroup: Poultry dishes — stir-fried or braised chicken with vegetables

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for onion and mushroom chicken. FOODON_03302051 (chicken dish) is the closest defensible parent class.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for onion and mushroom chicken. A00LC (chicken meat, cooked) is the closest defensible parent. Vegetable components (onion, mushroom) are not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for this specific prepared dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of onion and mushroom chicken in a Zi Char setting is approximately 300 g, intended for sharing among 2–3 diners as part of a multi-dish meal.

nutrient_source.title: Chicken, stir-fried with vegetables — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with IMR Malaysia MyFCD data for mushroom and onion components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / Institute for Medical Research Malaysia
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for onion and mushroom chicken exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD as a composite dish. Values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for cooked chicken (NDB 05064, chicken broilers, meat only, stir-fried) with proportional adjustments for onion (~15% by weight) and mushroom (~15% by weight) components using USDA FoodData Central entries for cooked onion (FDC 170000) and cooked mushroom (FDC 169251). Sauce contributions (oyster sauce, soy sauce, sesame oil) are estimated at ~5% by weight and increase sodium and fat marginally. This is a component-based estimation; confidence is medium.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; supplemented by University of Sydney GI Database entries for chicken and mixed vegetable dishes
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for onion and mushroom chicken. GI is estimated at 35 based on analogous mixed protein-vegetable stir-fry dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. Chicken (GI ~0 as a pure protein food) and non-starchy vegetables (onion GI ~10, mushroom GI ~10–15) contribute negligible glycemic load. The small amount of sauce (oyster sauce, soy sauce) adds minimal carbohydrate. The composite GI of 35 is a conservative estimate anchored to similar low-GI protein-dominant dishes. Glycemic load per 300 g serving = (35 × 5.4 × 3) / 100 = 5.67, rounded to 5.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from soy sauce and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should be aware that the soy sauce and oyster sauce used in this dish contribute approximately 420 mg sodium per 100 g; portion control and requesting reduced-sauce preparation are advisable to keep sodium intake within the recommended 2000 mg per day limit.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and high protein content
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a low estimated GI of 35 and a high protein content of approximately 14.8 g per 100 g, this dish is generally suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed as part of a balanced meal with non-starchy vegetables and a controlled carbohydrate staple such as brown rice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat and sodium from sauce ingredients
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish contains moderate saturated fat (~1.9 g per 100 g) and elevated sodium from condiments; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit portion size, request less sauce, and pair the dish with high-fibre sides to support a heart-healthy dietary pattern.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Onion and Mushroom Chicken (洋葱蘑菇鸡; Ayam Bawang Cendawan) is a popular Zi Char dish served in Malaysian and Singaporean Chinese restaurants and hawker-style eateries. The dish consists of bite-sized pieces of chicken — typically thigh or drumstick meat — stir-fried or lightly braised with sliced yellow onions and button or shiitake mushrooms in a savoury sauce made from oyster sauce, light soy sauce, sesame oil, garlic, and sometimes a small amount of cornstarch slurry to thicken the gravy. The result is a tender, juicy chicken dish with a rich umami flavour profile, slightly sweet from the caramelised onions and earthy from the mushrooms. The texture contrasts soft chicken meat with the slight bite of onion and the silky texture of cooked mushrooms. Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of lean protein (~14.8 g per 100 g), moderate in fat (~7.2 g per 100 g), and low in carbohydrates (~5.4 g per 100 g), making it a relatively balanced protein-centred dish. The estimated glycemic index is low (~35), suitable for blood glucose management. Sodium content is moderate to high due to soy-based condiments (~420 mg per 100 g), which is a consideration for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is commonly ordered as a shared plate in a multi-dish Zi Char meal alongside rice and other vegetable or seafood dishes. It is widely available across Singapore and Malaysia and is considered a home-style comfort dish.
common_user_queries: How many calories in onion and mushroom chicken?
Is onion mushroom chicken suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of chicken with mushroom and onion?
Is Zi Char chicken dish high in sodium?
Can I eat onion mushroom chicken if I have high blood pressure?

confidence_reason: No exact composite dish entry for onion and mushroom chicken was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy data for stir-fried chicken combined with proportional adjustments for onion and mushroom components. GI is an analogous estimate anchored to similar low-GI protein-vegetable dishes from the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the component-based estimation methodology.
notes: 1. Vitamin D is -1 as it is not reported in the source databases used for this dish type. 2. Trans fat is reported as 0.0 g per 100 g; this is confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy data for stir-fried chicken — no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional preparation. 3. Nutrient values represent a typical restaurant preparation; home-cooked versions may differ in sodium and fat content depending on sauce quantities used. 4. The food_group has been corrected from the input value of 'Vegetables' to 'Meat and alternatives' as chicken is the primary ingredient. 5. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the accessed date. 6. Halal status is marked true as chicken prepared without pork-derived ingredients is consistent with halal dietary requirements; individual restaurant certification should be verified. 7. Monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data and are not directly measured for this dish.
